region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Hunan influence); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — braised pork large intestine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pork large intestine food product class (FOODON_03302050); no specific braised spicy casserole preparation subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers pig large intestine as a raw commodity. No prepared braised casserole code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for braised pork intestine casserole as a prepared dish.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of spicy braised pork intestine casserole is approximately 300 g, representing a shared claypot portion for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Pork large intestine, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397) and adapted with braised preparation adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397 / FDC ID 168374). Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to account for braising with doubanjiang, soy sauce, chilli oil, and aromatics typical of this preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is the closest defensible approved-source anchor; no Singapore HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy braised pork intestine casserole. GI estimated at 32 based on the analogous category of cooked offal and organ meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content, referencing the low-GI classification for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625–1632). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (32 × 3.2 × 3) / 100 = 3.07, rounded to 3.1. GI category: low (<55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, doubanjiang, and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,560 mg per 300 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-the-side preparation may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated 32) result in a low glycemic load per serving, making this dish unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; however, the high fat and sodium content warrant attention to overall dietary balance in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium from pork intestine and braising condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork large intestine is inherently high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 132 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), and the braising process adds substantial sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Braised Pork Intestine Casserole (香辣肥肠煲, Xiāng là féi cháng bāo) is a bold, aromatic Chinese dish featuring cleaned and pre-cooked pork large intestine slow-braised in a richly spiced sauce. The dish is characteristic of Sichuan and Hunan culinary traditions and is popular in Chinese restaurants across Singapore. The intestines are first blanched and cleaned to remove impurities, then braised with doubanjiang (spicy fermented broad bean paste), dried chillies, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, and whole spices such as star anise, producing a deeply savory, spicy, and slightly numbing flavour profile. The texture is soft, glutinous, and satisfyingly chewy — a hallmark of well-prepared offal. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 14.8 g per 100 g) and fat (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g), with a relatively low carbohydrate content (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 182 kcal per 100 g. It is a significant source of dietary cholesterol and sodium, making it a dish of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 32), consistent with high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat preparations. This dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains soy and gluten allergens from the condiments used. It is typically served as a shared claypot dish in a restaurant setting, often accompanied by steamed white rice.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for spicy braised pork intestine casserole exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397) with adjustments for braising condiments. GI is estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed GI tables for low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397, cooked pork large intestine) with upward adjustment to sodium (~520 mg/100g) and fat to reflect braising with soy sauce, doubanjiang, and chilli oil. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4-9-4). 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. 7. GI is estimated at 32 (low) by analogy with cooked high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes per Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. This dish is not halal (contains pork) and not kosher. 9. Allergens include soy and gluten from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine. 10. The price listed in the source (SGD 49.40) likely reflects a full casserole for sharing rather than an individual portion.
